chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number:
CSCE 689
Course Title:
Computational Methods for Network Science
Time:
08:00am-09:15am, TR
Location:
HRBB 126
Credit Hours:
3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Numerical methods and optimization techniques prominent in graph analysis and network science;
eigenvector computations, spectral graph theory, matrix factorizations, constrained optimization
methods, gradient methods, linear least squares, graph-based clustering, centrality measures in network
analysis, approximation algorithms for graph problems

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: No formal prerequisites. Students should be generally familiar with undergraduate level linear algebra

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
•
Formulate common data and graph analysis tasks as optimization problems.
•
Identify and select appropriate algorithms and computational techniques to solve different
classes of optimization problems.
•
Analyze algorithms for solving optimization problems in network science and prove their
convergence properties.
•
Construct rigorous proofs to establish theoretical guarantees for algorithmic techniques
and refine proof-writing skills.
•
Evaluate the strengths, limitations, and pitfalls of different computational methods when
addressing practical tasks.
•
Examine and interpret specific network science problems and applications, and relate them to
the fundamental tools taught in the course.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
The course will be taught using a combination of lecture notes and selected portions of the following
textbooks:
Nocedal, Jorge, and Stephen J. Wright, eds. Numerical optimization. New York, NY: Springer
New York, 1999.
Wright, Stephen J., and Benjamin Recht. Optimization for data analysis. Cambridge University
Press, 2022.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
•
Quizzes
15%
•
Homework assignments
35%
•
Paper presentation
20%
• Final Project
35%
The grading scale will be A ≥ 90% > B > 80% > C ≥ 70% > D ≥ 60% > F.

chunks.title: Quizzes
chunks.content:
Quizzes will either be given in class as an exercise or will be assigned on Canvas. The purpose of quizzes
is to help you keep up with the material and be aware of things you are expected to know for the
course.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is
exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).
For every homework assignment, there will be a 6 hour grace period after the due time of the
homework. Any homework assignment turned in after the time it is due, but before the 6 hour grace
period has expired, will be accepted without penalty. Homework will not be accepted after the grace
period has expired.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
This schedule is tentative and subject to change.

| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| 1 | Intro to numerical linear algebra and graph theory |
| 2 | Fundamentals of unconstrained optimization |
| 3 | Unconstrained opt in network science: PageRank linear system |
| 4 | Eigenvalue and eigenvector computations |
| 5 | Spectral graph theory |
| 6 | Fundamentals of constrained optimization |
| 7 | Linear programming in network science |
| 8 | Vertex cover approximation algorithms |
| 9 | Minimum spanning tree-based clustering |
| 10 | Spring break |
| 11 | Graph partitioning and graph clustering |
| 12 | Special topics in network science |
| 13 | Special topics in network science |
| 14 | Student presentations and final project work |
| 15 | Student presentations and final project work |
| 16 | Student presentations and final project work |

chunks.title: Policy on Collaboration and Outside Resources
chunks.content:
Students are allowed, under the following guidelines, to consult outside sources and discuss homework
problems with other students.

(1) Writing. The write-up must be drafted by the student and in the student's own words. Students
should not have another person/entity do the writing of any substantive portion of an assignment
for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using artificial
intelligence tools like ChatGPT.
(2) Attribution. Students are required to list all resources and collaborators. This includes any
webpages, textbooks, discussions with other students in the course or people outside the course,
and any type of AI tool used (which should only be used subject to the AI guidelines below).
Discussing problems includes both receiving help or giving help of any kind.
(3) Not soliciting answers online. Posting homework questions or soliciting help in online sites or
forums is strictly prohibited. While reading an existing post or article about a similar problem is
allowed, creating a new post asking for the answer to your question is not allowed.

Some of this text is adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst GenAl syllabus statements.

chunks.title: Policy on Generative AI
chunks.content:
Students are allowed Generative Al tools (which includes ChatGPT) as long as they are used to gain
knowledge about a topic or problem and not used to simply generate answers which are turned in as
the student's own work. Al tools are considered outside resources and their use should follow the Policy
on Collaboration and Outside Resources. Additionally, use of Al tools is subject to the following
requirements:

•
Students are not allowed to directly copy-paste or otherwise directly upload any portion of the
class lecture notes or homework questions as a prompt to an AI tool. Note that the lecture notes
and homework sets are copyrighted material.
•
The student is responsible for writing up all solutions to their homework. Directly turning in text
generated by an AI tool, even if it is slightly modified, is a violation of academic integrity. Using
AI tools to spell-check, grammar-check, or slightly refine text written by the student (e.g., for
better clarity or conciseness) is permitted.
•
The student must acknowledge at the top of every homework whether AI tools were used, and if
so which ones.

The above guidelines specify what is allowed, but this is neither an encouragement nor an endorsement
of using Al tools in any way. All assignments in the class are explicitly designed to be solvable using only
the lectures and the textbook readings. Students are encouraged to simply not use Al tools to for this
particular course.

chunks.title: Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students
are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including
definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance
contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed
appropriate by the instructor.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions,
and related documentation and timelines.

Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more
than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the
student and instructor" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).

"The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed
because of an unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).

Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student
Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)
